pattern: Peeling a branching tree apart by depth turns it into a sequence of frontiers you can harvest one whole layer at a time.
hints: Recursion alone forces you to carry an extra depth parameter everywhere just to know which output row a node belongs to.
Track a frontier — the full set of nodes currently at the same depth — and record every value in it before replacing the frontier with the flattened list of their children.
Loop while the frontier is non-empty: read off its values as one output row, then rebuild the frontier from the concatenation of every current node's children list.

edge_inputs.description: an empty tree produces no rows at all
a lone root with no children is a single one-element row
one root fanning out to nine leaf children forms exactly two rows
a single-child chain six nodes deep, one node per row, tests a frontier that never grows
an irregular tree with uneven fan-out at different depths
repeated values across separate nodes so a row can't be deduplicated by value

stress.description: random n-ary trees grown by attaching each new node to a random existing node with up to 4 children
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 80)))
    children = {1: []}
    order = [1]
    for v in range(2, n + 1):
        candidates = [p for p in order if len(children[p]) < 4]
        parent = rng.choice(candidates) if candidates else rng.choice(order)
        children[parent].append(v)
        children[v] = []
        order.append(v)
    wire = [1, None]
    queue = [1]
    while queue:
        node = queue.pop(0)
        for k in children[node]:
            wire.append(k)
            queue.append(k)
        wire.append(None)
    while wire and wire[-1] is None:
        wire.pop()
    return [wire]
